True love for the people of God can be distinguished from natural kindness or party attachments by its being united with the love of God and obedience to his commands. The same Holy Spirit that taught love will have taught obedience also; and a person cannot truly love the children of God who, by habit, commits sin or neglects known duty. As God’s commands are holy, just, and good rules of liberty and happiness, those who are born of God and love him do not consider them grievous but lament that they cannot serve him more perfectly. Self-denial is required, but true Christians have a principle which carries them above all hindrances. Though the conflict often is sharp, and regenerate people may be cast down, yet he will rise up and renew his combat with resolution. But all, except believers in Christ, are enslaved in some respect to the customs, opinions, or interests of the world. Faith is the cause of victory, the means, the instrument, the spiritual armor by which we overcome. In and by faith we cleave to Christ, in contempt of, and in opposition to the world. Faith sanctifies the heart and purifies it from those sensual lusts by which the world obtains sway and dominion over souls. It has the indwelling Spirit of grace, which is greater than he who dwells in the world. The real Christian overcomes the world by faith; he sees, in and by the life and conduct of the Lord Jesus on earth, that this world is to be renounced and overcome. He cannot be satisfied with this world but looks beyond it and is still tending, striving, and pressing toward heaven. We must all, after Christ’s example, overcome the world or it will overcome us to our ruin.

We are inwardly and outwardly defiled; inwardly, by the power and pollution of sin in our nature. For our cleansing there is in and by Christ Jesus, the washing of regeneration and the renewing of the Holy Spirit. Some think that the two sacraments are here meant: baptism with water, as the outward sign of regeneration, and purifying from the pollution of sin by the Holy Spirit; and the Lord’s supper, as the outward sign of the shedding of Christ's blood, and receiving him by faith for pardon and justification. Both these ways of cleansing were represented in the old ceremonial sacrifices and cleansings. This water and blood include all that is necessary to our salvation. By the water, our souls are washed and purified for heaven and the habitation of saints in light. By the blood, we are justified, reconciled, and presented righteous to God. By the blood, the curse of the law being satisfied, the purifying Spirit is obtained for the internal cleansing of our natures. The water, as well as the blood, came out of the side of the sacrificed Redeemer. He loved the church, and gave himself for it, that he might sanctify and cleanse it with the washing of water by the word; that he might present it to himself a glorious church. This was done in and by the Spirit of God, according to the Savior’s declaration. He is the Spirit of God, and cannot lie. Three had borne witness to these doctrines concerning the person and the salvation of Christ. The Father, repeatedly, by a voice from heaven declared that Jesus was his beloved Son. The Word declared that He and the Father were One, and that whoever has seen him has seen the Father. And the Holy Spirit, who descended from heaven and rested on Christ at his baptism; who had borne witness to Him by all the prophets; and gave testimony to his resurrection and mediatorial office, by the gift of miraculous powers to the apostles. But whether this passage be cited or not, the doctrine of the Trinity in Unity stands equally firm and certain. To the doctrine taught by the apostles respecting the person and salvation of Christ, there were three testimonies. 1. The Holy Spirit. We come into the world with a corrupt, carnal disposition, which is enmity to God. This being done away by the regeneration and new-creating of souls by the Holy Spirit, is a testimony to the Savior. 2. The water: this sets forth the Savior's purity and purifying power. The actual and active purity and holiness of his disciples are represented by baptism. 3. The blood which he shed: and this was our ransom; this testifies for Jesus Christ; it sealed up and finished the sacrifices of the Old Testament. The benefits procured by his blood prove that he is the Savior of the world. No wonder if he who rejects this evidence is judged a blasphemer of the Spirit of God. These three witnesses are for one and the same purpose; they agree in one and the same thing.

Nothing could be more absurd than the conduct of those who doubt the truth of Christianity, while in everyday matters they do not hesitate to rely on human testimony, and would consider anyone foolish who declined to do so. The true Christian has seen his guilt and misery, and his need for such a Savior. He has seen how suitable such a Savior is to all his spiritual needs and circumstances. He has found and felt the power of Christ’s word and teachings, humbling, healing, quickening, and comforting his soul. He has a new disposition and new joys, and is not the person he used to be. Yet he still finds conflict with himself, with sin, with the flesh, the world, and wicked powers. But he finds such strength from faith in Christ that he can overcome the world and move forward toward something better. This is the assurance the gospel believer has: he has a witness within himself, which settles the matter for him, except during times of darkness or conflict; but he cannot be persuaded out of his belief in the main truths of the gospel. Here is what makes the unbeliever’s sin so terrible: the sin of disbelief. He gives God the lie because he does not believe the record that God gave about his Son. It is pointless for a person to claim that he believes God's testimony in other matters while rejecting it in this. Whoever refuses to trust and honor Christ as the Son of God, who rejects submitting to his teaching as Prophet, relying on his atonement and intercession as High Priest, or obeying him as King, is dead in sin, under condemnation; nor will any outward morality, learning, forms, ideas, or confidence help him.

Based on all this evidence, it is right that we believe in the name of the Son of God. Believers have eternal life within the agreement of the gospel. Then let us thankfully receive the record of Scripture. Let us always be eager in the work of the Lord, knowing that our effort is not wasted in the Lord. The Lord Christ invites us to come to him in all situations, with our requests and needs, even though sin surrounds us. Our prayers must always be offered in submission to God’s will. Sometimes they are quickly answered; at other times they are granted in the best way, even if not as requested. We should pray for others, as well as for ourselves. There are sins that fight against spiritual life within the soul and the life above. We cannot pray that the sins of those who do not repent or believe should be forgiven while they remain so; or that mercy, which assumes forgiveness of sins, should be granted to them while they stubbornly continue in this state. But we may pray for their repentance, for being filled with faith in Christ, and then for all other saving mercies. We should pray for others, as well as for ourselves, asking the Lord to pardon and restore those who have fallen, as well as to relieve the tempted and afflicted. And let us be truly thankful that no sin, of which anyone genuinely repents, leads to death.

All mankind are divided into two groups: those who belong to God, and those who belong to the wicked one. True believers belong to God; they are from him, and to him, and for him. The rest, by far the greater number, are in the power of the wicked one; they do his works and support his cause. This statement includes all unbelievers, whatever their profession, status, or circumstances, or by whatever name they may be called. The Son leads believers to the Father, and they are in the love and favor of both; united with both, by the indwelling and working of the Holy Spirit. Happy are those to whom it is given to know that the Son of God has come, and to have a heart to trust in and rely on him who is true! May this be our privilege; we will thus be kept from all idols and false doctrines, and from the idolatrous love of worldly objects, and be kept by the power of God, through faith, unto eternal salvation. To this living and true God, be glory and dominion forever and ever. Amen.
